admin_checkerHelpBody: Your validator follows the DOMjudge output-validator interface and runs in its own isolated container — the student's program never sees the answer. It is invoked as `validator <input> <judge_answer> <feedback_dir>` with the student output on stdin. Python: `judge_input`, `judge_answer`, `team_output` are pre-bound as strings, and helpers `accept(team_msg="")`, `wrong(team_msg="")`, `set_score(x)`, `judge_log(msg)` write the right feedback files and exit. C++: read `argv[1]` / `argv[2]` and stdin yourself, write `score.txt` / `teammessage.txt` into the `argv[3]` feedback dir, and exit 42 (accept) or 43 (wrong) — no judge header required. Feedback is shown to the student. Validator timeout is 30 seconds; a crash or hang becomes a system error and does not count against the student.
admin_containerContractHint: The TA’s image is fully self-contained: testcases and grading scripts must be bundled into the image. The system only passes in student files and reads the result you write.
admin_convertToAdvancedDesc: Advanced Mode gives you full control over the judge container, testcase flow, and judge script — ideal for custom grading needs.
admin_convertToAdvancedHint: Need a custom grading environment or a more complex workflow? You can convert this problem to Advanced Mode.
admin_convertToAdvancedInlineWarning: ⚠️ This will DELETE all workspace files, testcases, and judge settings — it cannot be undone.
admin_convertToAdvancedWarningHeader: ⚠️ Warning: this action cannot be undone
admin_convertToAdvancedWarningItem1: All workspace files will be deleted
admin_convertToAdvancedWarningItem2: All testcase sets and testcases will be deleted
admin_convertToAdvancedWarningItem3: Judge config and scoring rules will be reset
admin_deleteProblemMessage: Are you sure you want to delete this problem? This action cannot be undone. All testcases, submissions, and related data will be permanently removed.
admin_downloadStarterHint: A ready-to-build zip: edit grader.py, run docker build, then upload.
admin_exitCodeWarning: Non-zero exit code → runtime_error; missing {file} or invalid format → SE.
admin_helpVisibility:
Public: visible in problem list, anyone can submit.
Private: only the author, admins, and course members with access can view and submit.
admin_imageRefHint: e.g. {example} — the worker will pull this at grading time.
admin_imageRefRequired: A registry image reference is required.
admin_inputFormatTooltip: Describe the input format. Supports Markdown.
admin_interactorHelpBody: Your interactor follows the DOMjudge interactive interface and runs in its own isolated container, wired to the student program by a byte proxy: student stdout flows to interactor stdin, interactor stdout flows to student stdin. It is invoked as `interactor <input> <judge_answer> <feedback_dir>`. Python: `judge_input` and `judge_answer` are pre-bound strings; `read()` reads one line from the student (auto-rejecting if the stream closes) and `write(msg)` sends one line back (already flushed). Helpers `accept(team_msg="")`, `wrong(team_msg="")`, `set_score(x)`, `judge_log(msg)` write the feedback files and exit 42/43. C++: read `argv[1]`/`argv[2]`, talk to the solution over `cin`/`cout` (flush before reading), write `score.txt` / `teammessage.txt` into `argv[3]`, and exit 42 (accept) or 43 (wrong) — no judge header required. The student and interactor share the problem's time limit.
admin_judgeImageHint: Provide a Docker image. The container must bundle its own testcases and grading logic.

latePenalty_dailyDesc: Linear decay: lose N% per day. One day late = N%, two days late = 2N%, floored at zero.
latePenalty_finalDayZeroDesc: Submissions after the final day are scored zero. Hardest cutoff, suits exam-style work.
latePenalty_flatDesc: Late submissions lose a fixed percentage of the final score, regardless of how late.
latePenalty_noneDesc: Late submissions are not penalized. Use with a hard final-day cutoff.
legal_privacyContent:
NOJV is an online judge platform for programming courses, contests, and practice. This page describes what we collect, why we collect it, and what you can do about it.

## What we collect

- **Account data**: your email and password hash; if you sign in with GitHub or Google, the email and account ID returned by that provider
- **Profile**: your display name and avatar
- **Submissions**: the code you submit, compile and execution output, stdout and stderr, execution time, and memory usage
- **Activity**: your attempts, scores, and progress across courses, assignments, exams, and contests
- **Exam environment**: IP address during an exam, start and end timestamps, and any IP-lock violations
- **Posts**: announcements you publish and clarifications you ask, with their edit history

We do not collect browser fingerprints, run ad trackers, or perform cross-site analytics.

## How we use it

- Judging, scoring, and displaying results to people who are allowed to see them
- Plagiarism detection: when enabled, we send your submitted code to Stanford's MOSS service for similarity comparison
- Operations: debugging, security, and abuse prevention

## Who can see your data

- You can always see all of your own data
- In a course you take, the instructor and teaching assistants can see member rosters, submissions, and scores for that course
- Platform admins can see all data, used only for system operations
- We do not sell your data to third parties or share it outside the course context

## Third-party services

- **GitHub OAuth, Google OAuth**: when you sign in with either, we redirect you to that provider for authentication
- **MOSS (Stanford Measure Of Software Similarity)**: when plagiarism detection is enabled, your code is sent to MOSS for comparison
- **Object storage (GCS / R2 / S3 / MinIO)**: stores problem images and other static assets

## Retention and deletion

We retain your data while your account exists. To delete your account or specific records, email `contact@example.com`.

## Cookies

We use only cookies required for sign-in (session and CSRF). No analytics or advertising cookies.

## Contact

`contact@example.com`
legal_termsContent:
By registering for or using NOJV, you agree to these terms.

## The service

NOJV is an open-source online judge for educational use. We provide the service as-is and do our best to keep it running, but we make no guarantees about availability, judging accuracy, or data preservation. We may modify, suspend, or shut down the service when needed.

## Your account

- Keep your password safe and do not share your account
- Use one account per person, and do not impersonate anyone
- Tell us if you notice unauthorized access

## Acceptable use

Please do not:

- Upload malware, attempt to escape the judge sandbox, or attack the judging system or other users
- Make bulk automated requests or carry out denial-of-service attacks
- Violate exam supervision rules during an exam; IP locks, time limits, and similar violations are recorded
- Post off-topic, promotional, harassing, or unlawful content in announcements or clarifications

## Rights to code and content

- You retain copyright in code you submit
- By submitting, you license us to store, execute, judge, display to authorized viewers, and submit to plagiarism detection — only as needed to run the service
- The platform itself is open source; see the LICENSE on [GitHub](https://github.com/your-org/nojv)

## Minors

If you are under 18, please use the service with parental or instructor consent.

## Termination

We may terminate your account for violations or legal reasons. You can ask us to delete your account at any time.

## Disclaimer

To the maximum extent permitted by law, we are not liable for any losses arising from your use of the service, including but not limited to data loss and grading disputes.

## Changes

We may update these terms. Material changes will be announced on the site. Continued use means you accept the new terms.

## Governing law

These terms are governed by the laws of the Republic of China (Taiwan).

## Contact

`contact@example.com`
matrix_roleFilterTooltip: Role filter — placeholder, matrix currently shows only students
members_addDescription: Paste a list of handles (one per line, or comma-separated). The system will parse each handle and attach the matching student. Unknown handles become placeholders that auto-merge when the student first logs in with OAuth.
members_hint: No email will be sent and no join link is generated.
members_placeholderFooter: Placeholder rows are visually dimmed (? avatar, empty name) so you can see at a glance who hasn't signed in yet.
members_roleTaDesc: Can edit assignments, view class stats.
mobile_workspaceBlockerDescription: The code editor and submit form are disabled on small screens. Please switch to a desktop browser to solve this problem.
mobile_workspaceBlockerTitle: Use a desktop browser to solve
onboarding_schoolOptionDesc: Verify with your NTNU / NTU / NTUST email
onboarding_subtitle: Choose how to set your username
onboarding_usernamePatternError: Use 3-64 lowercase letters, digits, dots, hyphens, or underscores.
onboarding_usernamePatternHint: 3-64 characters, lowercase letters, digits, dots, hyphens, underscores
onboarding_usernameReserved: This username format is reserved for school accounts.
onboarding_verificationSent: Verification email sent! Check your inbox and click the link.
override_staff_reasonMaxError: Reason must be 500 characters or less
override_student_markerTooltip: This score has been manually adjusted by a teacher.
problemDetail_checkerHelp: Your stdout is handed to a custom checker script, which decides correctness. The expected output doesn't have to match byte-for-byte — useful for problems with multiple valid answers.
problemDetail_fullSourceHelp: Submit a complete program including main(). The judge compiles and runs your code against stdin and compares stdout against the expected output.
problemDetail_interactiveHelp: Your program talks back and forth with an interactor over stdin/stdout. Each reply from the interactor depends on your previous output — no fixed input/output file.
problemDetail_multiFileHelp: The problem ships multiple files — one or more you edit plus read-only helpers (and sometimes hidden files). You edit the designated files in the browser and submit them together.
problemDetail_specialEnvHelp: Judging runs entirely inside a teacher-provided Docker image. You upload a tarball containing your submission and the image decides how to build, run, and grade it. Because the image is the single source of truth, the usual judging-method badges (standard / checker / interactive) don't apply here.
problemDetail_standardHelp: Your stdout is compared line by line against the expected output. Trailing whitespace is normalised, but otherwise the comparison is exact.
problems_createAdvancedDescription: TA provides a Docker image that handles grading
problems_myProblemsEmpty: You haven't created any problems yet.
problems_subtitle: Browse and solve problems. Filter by difficulty, tags, or search by title.
submissionDetail_noCaseResults: No per-testcase breakdown is available for this submission.
submissionDetail_sampleOnlyNotice: This was a Run (sample-only) submission, not a scored submission.
submissionDetail_viewingAsStaff: You are viewing this submission as course staff.
submissions_emptyHint: Solve your first problem to see your submission history.
submissions_workspaceHint: Submissions live inside the problem workspace — open a problem to see your verdicts, runtimes and sources.
testcases_errorInvalidRegex: Invalid regex syntax. Please check the pattern.
testcases_errorNoFilesMatched: No files in the ZIP matched the current rules. Ensure file names match the regex {regex} with extensions {inExt} / {outExt}.
testcases_hiddenCasesHint: Used for grading only. Not visible to students.
testcases_scoringStrategyHint: All-or-nothing: any failing testcase makes the subtask score 0. Proportional: subtask score = (passing cases / total) × weight. Minimum: subtask score = min(per-case scores) × weight / 100, useful when a checker reports partial scores.
testcases_uploadZipHint: Upload a ZIP file containing testcases
testcases_zipFormatHelp:
ZIP structure: each testcase is a pair of files sharing the same base name.
Example: 0101.in / 0101.out, 0102.in / 0102.out, 0201.in / 0201.out …
The first capture group in the regex identifies the subtask, the second identifies the case.
Supported: up to 20 subtasks, 256 cases per subtask, 1 MB per file. Max ZIP size: 50 MB.
testcases_zipStructureHint:
Regex with 2 capture groups:
(1) subtask ID, (2) case ID.
Default (\d\d)(\d\d) matches fixed 4-digit names, e.g. 0103.in (subtask 01, case 03).
For names with a separator like 1-01.in, use (\d+)-(\d+) instead.
testcases_zipTooLarge: ZIP file too large ({size} MB). Maximum is 50 MB.
upload_dragDropHint: Drop a .zip archive or a single source file, or click to browse
verifySchool_successMessage: Your NOJV account has been set to {username}. You can close this page.
virtualContest_emptyDescription: This contest has no problems to replay.
virtualContest_heroHint: Replay {title} on your own clock and see how you would have ranked.
virtualContest_notEndedYet: A virtual contest can only be started after the contest ends.
virtualContest_startBlurb: Start a personal timed run of this past contest. Your timer matches the original contest duration.
workspace_descriptionPlaceholder: Describe what this file is for…
workspace_mustHaveMainFile: Each enabled language must have an editable file named {filename}
